content.full_text:
Re:
Fr,rn Porrrrca.r PnacrrcBs ColrvrrssroN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street . Sacramenro. CA 9581:-0807
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
September 5,2001
Steven Maviglio
Office of the Governor
Sacramento, CA 95814
Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No. I-01-189
Dear Mr. Maviglio
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 BecausJ your question does not concern a specific
govemmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance-2 This
letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have already taken
place. Finally, our response is based on the facts presented. The Commission does not
act as a finder of fact in its advice-giving capacity. (In re ogtesby (1975) I FppC ops.
7t.)
QUESTION
Does ownership of shares of stock in Enron Corporation represent a violation of
the state's conflict of interest laws?
CONCLUSION
No. Nothing in the Act prohibits the ownership of stock in any company
FACTS
You serve as the governor's press secretary and you are also filling in temporarily
as interim communications director. Your responsibilities include communicating the
I Government Code sections 8 I 000 - 9 l0 14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18-997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not prorid" the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (g 8311a; Reg. 18329, subd. (c)(3).)


File No. I-01-189
Page No. 2
governor's decisions and statements to the press; planning and producing media coverage
for the governor's events; and developing communications strategy for the governor's
programs.
You state your position does not involve making policy decisions. As the
governor's spokesperson, you communicate the policy decisions made by the governor
and the administration.
You hold 200 shares of Enron Corp., which are held in your Individual
Retirement Account (IRA). You have not taded in this stock since you purchased it five
years ago. You have not had any dealings with the governor's offrce, nor any knowledge
ofenergy purchases or contracts.
ANALYSN
The Political Reform Act nowhere prohibits ownership of any given stock or
other investment interest. Rather, the Act states in Government Code Section 87100:
"No public official at any level of state or local govemment shall
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a govemmental decision in which he knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest."
Thus, the mere fact, alone, of ownership3 of Enron stock does not give rise to a conflict of
interest for you.
Instead, potential conflicts of interest arise where there is a confluence of an
economic interest with a govemmental decision. To implement Section 87100, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step analysis to determine whether a public official
may have a conflict of interest. (Reg. 18700, copy enclosed.)a One of the elements in
determining the presence or absence of a conflict of interest is the identification of a
govemmental decision in which the official will make, participate in or influence. (Regs.
18700 - 18708.) Because you have not identified any future or pending decision in which
you may participate, we cannot advise whether.you may or may not have a conflict of
interest by virtue of your ownership of Enron stock and the specific performance of your
duties.
3 An investment interest worth $2,000 triggers a potential conflict of interest. ($ $ 82034, 87 103,
subd. (a)) Presumably this threshold is met in your case.
' Step One asks whether the individual is a public official. Step Two asks whether the official
will be making, participating in making or using or attempting to use his or her position to influence a
decision. Step Three identifies the public offrcial's economic interests. Step Four determines whether the
given interest identified in Step Three is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision. Steps
Five and Six apply the materiality standard appropriate for the given type of economic interest at issue and
determines whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have a material
financial effect on the economic interest. (Reg. 18700.) Steps Seven and Eight are exceptions to the
conflicts provisions, to be considered in the event a conflict ofinterest arises.


File No. I-01-189
Page No. 3
While we cannot advise whether you have a ionflict of interest as discussed
above, your letter suggests that, since you are not involved in policy decisions, your
position as a spokesf"rro., could not present a scenario in which a conflict might arise.s
We can only, however, render advice and conclusions with respect to specific decisions
which the public offrcial may make, participate in or influence in the context of his or her
duties. A critical element in the process of determining whether a conflict of interest
exists, step two, is whether the public official is making, participating in making or
influencing a govemmental decision. (Regs. 18700 - 18708.) You state you don't make
decisions. As indicated above, however, we do not have sufficient facts to conclude
whether you are participating in or influencing a govemmental decision and therefore
cannot advise whether you have a potentially disqualiffing conflict of interest with
respect to the Enron stock.
You are encouraged to write the Commission for further advice when and if more
specific facts arise. In the meantime, I have enclosed afact sheet designed to help public
officials determine whether they may have a conflict of interest. If you have any other
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa
By:
C. Scott Tocher
Counsel, Legal Division
CSTjg
I:\Adviceltrs\0 1 - I 89
' The only exception to this statement is where the individual is not a "public official" under the
Act and therefore not subject to the prohibition of Section 87 I 00.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does ownership of shares of stock in Enron Corporation represent a violation of
the state's conflict of interest laws?

CONCLUSION: No. Nothing in the Act prohibits the ownership of stock in any company
